topics.title_ko: 팀과 기술적 결정에서 의견이 갈렸던 경험을 말해보세요

A2.title: Tell me about a time you disagreed with your team on a technical decision.
A2.body: Last year, my team had to choose a database for a new project. Most people wanted to use the same database we always used. I thought a different one was better for our needs. I was worried, but I spoke up in a meeting. I shared my reasons with simple examples. Some people did not agree at first. So I built a small test to show the speed. After they saw the test, they agreed. We changed our plan, and the project worked well. I learned that it is important to speak up, but with proof.

B1.title: Tell me about a time you disagreed with your team on a technical decision.
B1.body:
A few months ago, my team had to choose a database for a new service. Most of my teammates wanted to use the same one we always used, because it felt safe and familiar. I felt strongly that a different database would be a better fit for the kind of data we were storing.

It wasn't easy to disagree, especially with people who had more experience. But instead of just arguing, I built a small test project over a weekend. I showed the team real numbers — query times, memory use, and a clear comparison.

After they saw the results, the conversation changed completely. They started asking practical questions, and in the end we agreed to use the new database. The launch went smoothly, and the service is still running on it.

What I took away is that disagreement is fine — even useful — as long as you bring evidence, not just opinions.

key_phrases.meaning_ko: 의견 차이는 괜찮다, 심지어 유용하다 (em-dash 강조)

B2.title: Tell me about a time you disagreed with your team on a technical decision.
B2.body:
A few months ago, my team had to choose the database for a new internal service. Most of my teammates wanted to stick with the database we always used, mainly because everyone was already comfortable with it. I, on the other hand, was convinced that a different database would handle our access patterns much better, especially as the data grew.

Pushing back wasn't comfortable. A few of my teammates were more senior than me, and I didn't want to come across as inexperienced or arrogant. So I decided that simply repeating my opinion wouldn't be enough.

Over the weekend, I built a small proof-of-concept and ran realistic benchmarks against both options. I prepared a one-page summary with query times, memory usage, and operational trade-offs, and I made sure to acknowledge the downsides of my preferred option as well.

When I shared it in the next meeting, the tone of the discussion shifted. We stopped talking about which database we liked and started talking about which one fit the problem. We ended up going with my recommendation.

The launch went smoothly, and the service has been running on that database for several months with no major issues. What I took away from the experience is that healthy disagreement isn't about winning the argument — it's about replacing opinions with evidence so the team can decide together.

C1.title: Tell me about a time you disagreed with your team on a technical decision.
C1.body:
A few months ago, my team had to choose the database that would back a new internal service. Most of my teammates instinctively wanted to stick with the database we had used for years — partly out of familiarity, and partly because it had already been operationally tuned to our environment. I, however, had become increasingly convinced that a different database would handle our particular access patterns far better, especially as the dataset scaled.

Pushing back wasn't comfortable. Several of my teammates had more experience than I did, and I was conscious of not wanting to come across as inexperienced or, worse, arrogant. So I decided early on that simply restating my opinion would get me nowhere.

Over a weekend, I built a small proof-of-concept and benchmarked both options against workloads that closely resembled what production would look like. I distilled the results into a one-page memo: query latencies, memory footprints, operational trade-offs, and — importantly — an honest account of the weaknesses of my preferred option.

When I shared the document in our next design meeting, the conversation changed almost immediately. We stopped debating which database we liked more and started reasoning about which one actually fit the problem in front of us. By the end of the meeting, we had aligned on my recommendation, and several teammates told me afterwards that the structured comparison had made it much easier to engage with the proposal.

The service launched on schedule and has been running on the new database without serious incident ever since. What I took away from the experience was a lesson I keep returning to: healthy disagreement, especially with more senior colleagues, is rarely won by repeating yourself more loudly. It's won by quietly replacing opinions with evidence and giving the team something concrete enough to push back on.

key_phrases.phrase: was conscious of not wanting to
a lesson I keep returning to

A2.title: Do you enjoy cooking?
A2.body: Yes, I do. I like cooking simple meals on the weekend. My favorite thing to make is fried rice, because it is fast and tastes good. During the week, I am busy, so I cook less. But on Sundays, I really enjoy it.

key_phrases.phrase: my favorite thing to make is

B1.title: Do you enjoy cooking?
B1.body: Yes, I really enjoy cooking, although I have to admit I'm not very good at it yet. I usually cook on the weekends, because I'm too tired during the week. My favorite thing to make is fried rice — it's quick, you can use almost anything in the fridge, and it always tastes good. Cooking helps me relax after a busy week.

key_phrases.phrase: I'm not very good at it yet

B2.title: Do you enjoy cooking?
B2.body: Yes, I do enjoy cooking, even though I'd describe myself as more of a casual cook than a skilled one. During the week I tend to keep things simple, but on weekends I like to try something a bit more interesting, like a new pasta sauce or a recipe I've seen online. What I enjoy most is the calmness of it — chopping vegetables and following a recipe is a nice way to slow down after a stressful week at work. Also, it's quite satisfying to eat something you made yourself.

key_phrases.phrase: more of an X than a Y

C1.title: Do you enjoy cooking?
C1.body: Yes, I genuinely enjoy cooking, although I'd hesitate to call myself a serious cook. During the week I usually keep things straightforward — something quick that doesn't take much thought after a long day. On weekends, though, I like to be a bit more adventurous, whether that means trying a new pasta sauce or attempting a recipe I've come across online. Honestly, what draws me to it isn't really the food itself; it's the calmness of the process. Chopping vegetables, following a recipe step by step — there's something almost meditative about it after a stressful week at the screen. And of course, there's a quiet kind of satisfaction in sitting down to eat something you made with your own hands.

key_phrases.phrase: what draws me to it isn't really
key_phrases.meaning_ko: 내가 거기에 끌리는 건 사실 ~이 아니다

key_phrases.phrase: there's something almost X about it

A2.title: Describe a skill you would like to learn. You should say: what the skill is, why you want to learn it, how you would learn it, and explain why it would be useful for you.
A2.body:
I would like to learn how to play the guitar. I have wanted to do this for a long time, but I never had the time.

I want to learn guitar for many reasons. First, I love listening to music. Some songs sound so beautiful with guitar. Second, my friend can play, and it looks fun.

To learn, I would buy a cheap guitar first. I do not want to spend a lot of money before I know if I really like it. Then I would watch videos online. There are many free lessons on YouTube. I would also try to play a little every day, maybe twenty or thirty minutes.

This skill would be useful for me because I want to relax more. My job is on a computer all day. Music with my hands would be very different. Also, I could play for friends. That would be a nice way to spend time with them.

B1.title: Describe a skill you would like to learn. You should say: what the skill is, why you want to learn it, how you would learn it, and explain why it would be useful for you.
B1.body:
A skill I would really like to learn is playing the guitar. It's something I've been thinking about for years, but I've always told myself I don't have time.

There are a few reasons I want to learn. First, I genuinely love music, and most of my favorite songs have guitar in them. Second, one of my close friends plays the guitar, and watching him casually play after dinner always looks so relaxing and fun. I think it would be a great way to connect with him too.

If I really decided to do it, I'd probably start by buying a cheap second-hand guitar, because I don't want to spend a lot of money before I'm sure I'll stick with it. Then I would use free lessons on YouTube — there are so many good teachers online. I'd try to practice a little every day, maybe twenty or thirty minutes, instead of one long session on the weekend.

This skill would be useful for me in a few ways. My job is in front of a computer all day, so picking up something that uses my hands would be a really nice change. It would also help me relax, because I get stressed quite easily. And honestly, it would just be fun. At my age, learning a new skill that has nothing to do with work feels almost like a small gift to myself.

key_phrases.phrase: feels like a small gift to myself

B2.title: Describe a skill you would like to learn. You should say: what the skill is, why you want to learn it, how you would learn it, and explain why it would be useful for you.
B2.body:
A skill I would really like to learn is playing the guitar. It's something I've been thinking about for years, but I've always managed to convince myself I don't have time for it.

There are several reasons I want to learn. The most basic one is that I genuinely love music — a huge proportion of my favorite songs are built around the guitar, and there's something about the instrument's warmth that I find emotionally appealing. On top of that, one of my closest friends plays the guitar quite well, and whenever I see him casually pick it up after dinner, it always looks both relaxing and impressive. I imagine it would also give us a new way to spend time together.

If I were to actually commit to learning, I'd probably start by buying a cheap second-hand acoustic, because I don't want to invest a lot of money before I know whether I'll stick with it. Then I would rely mostly on YouTube tutorials, since there are excellent teachers offering structured beginner courses for free. Rather than practicing in long, intense sessions on the weekend, I'd try to play for twenty or thirty minutes every day, because I believe consistency matters far more than intensity when it comes to learning an instrument.

This skill would be useful for me in several ways. My work involves staring at a computer screen for most of the day, so taking up something that engages my hands and ears in a completely different way would be a welcome change. It would also help me unwind, since I tend to carry stress home with me. And perhaps most importantly, at this stage of life, deliberately learning something that has absolutely nothing to do with my career feels like a small but meaningful gift to myself.

key_phrases.phrase: If I were to actually commit to

C1.title: Describe a skill you would like to learn. You should say: what the skill is, why you want to learn it, how you would learn it, and explain why it would be useful for you.
C1.body:
A skill I would genuinely like to learn is playing the guitar. To be honest, it's something I've been quietly thinking about for years, but I've always managed to talk myself out of it on the grounds that I don't have the time — which, in retrospect, sounds suspiciously like an excuse.

There are several reasons the guitar appeals to me. The most obvious one is that I love music — a disproportionate number of my favorite songs are built around the instrument, and there's something about its warmth and intimacy that I find emotionally compelling in a way that, say, a piano never quite reaches for me. Beyond that, one of my closest friends plays remarkably well, and whenever he casually picks up his guitar after dinner, it always strikes me as both relaxing for him and quietly impressive to watch. I suspect learning would also give the two of us a completely new way of spending time together.

If I ever actually committed to learning, I think I'd start in a deliberately modest way. I'd buy a cheap second-hand acoustic, mainly because I don't want to pour money into an expensive instrument before I'm convinced I'll stick with it. From there, I'd rely heavily on YouTube, where there are surprisingly good teachers offering structured beginner courses entirely for free. Rather than attempting long, ambitious practice sessions on the weekend, I'd aim for twenty or thirty minutes a day, on the principle that consistency matters far more than intensity when you're learning an instrument from scratch.

This skill would be useful to me in ways that go beyond simply being able to play a few songs. My job involves staring at a screen for most of the day, so taking up something that engages my hands and ears in an entirely different mode would be a meaningful counterweight. It would also, I suspect, help me unwind, because I tend to bring work-related stress home with me without realizing it. And perhaps most importantly of all, at this point in life, deliberately learning something that has absolutely nothing to do with my career feels less like a hobby and more like a quiet investment in the kind of person I want to remain.

key_phrases.phrase: feels less like X and more like Y

topics.title_ko: 사람들이 일을 위해 필요한 기술이 변하고 있다고 보나요? 어떻게요?

A2.title: Do you think the kinds of skills people need for work are changing? In what ways?
A2.body: Yes, I think they are changing a lot. In the past, people did the same job for a long time. Now, jobs change quickly. Many people use computers and the internet every day at work. So, computer skills are important now. Also, people work with others from different countries, so English is helpful. I think we will have to learn new things many times in our lives.

B1.title: Do you think the kinds of skills people need for work are changing? In what ways?
B1.body:
Yes, I definitely think the kinds of skills people need at work are changing quite quickly. In the past, many people did the same job for thirty or forty years. These days, that is becoming rare. Most jobs now require some level of digital skills, even simple ones like sending emails or using shared documents online.

Another big change is that we work much more with people from other countries. So good communication in English, and the ability to work in different cultures, has become very important. I also think we are expected to keep learning new tools all the time. I really believe that being open to learning, more than any specific skill, will be the most important thing in the future.

B2.title: Do you think the kinds of skills people need for work are changing? In what ways?
B2.body:
Yes, I think the skills required for most jobs are changing quite dramatically, and at a much faster pace than even ten years ago. The most obvious shift is digital. Even roles that aren't considered technical now expect employees to be comfortable with shared documents, video calls, data dashboards, and increasingly, AI tools. Someone who refuses to learn these tends to find themselves slowly pushed to the margins of their team.

A second change is that work has become much more global. Many of us collaborate with colleagues in completely different time zones, which means soft skills like clear written English, cultural awareness, and the ability to communicate asynchronously are now genuinely valuable, not just nice to have.

But the deeper change, in my view, isn't any specific skill — it's the expectation that you will keep learning throughout your career. The half-life of a particular tool or framework is shrinking every year, so what matters most is the underlying habit of being open to learning. In the end, I'd argue that adaptability has quietly become the most important skill of all, because it's the one that lets you acquire all the others.

C1.title: Do you think the kinds of skills people need for work are changing? In what ways?
C1.body:
Yes, I'd argue that the skills required for most kinds of work are changing fairly dramatically, and at a noticeably faster pace than even a decade ago. The most visible shift is digital. Even roles that aren't traditionally considered technical — teaching, healthcare, marketing, customer support — now assume a baseline comfort with shared documents, video conferencing, data dashboards, and, increasingly, with AI tools. People who actively resist learning these tend to find themselves quietly pushed to the margins of their teams, almost without anyone deciding it should happen.

A second, less talked-about shift is that work has become genuinely global in a way it simply wasn't twenty years ago. Many of us now collaborate with colleagues in entirely different time zones, which means a set of softer skills — writing clearly in English, navigating cultural differences, and communicating effectively without immediate face-to-face feedback — have become substantively valuable, rather than merely admirable.

The deepest change, though, isn't any one particular skill. It's the expectation that learning is no longer something you finish, but something you do continuously. The effective half-life of a specific tool or framework keeps shrinking, which means what really matters is the underlying habit of staying curious and being willing to start from zero on something new every few years. In that sense, I'd argue that adaptability has quietly become the most important skill of our time — not because any single role demands it, but because it's the meta-skill that allows you to acquire whatever the next era of work happens to require.

vocab.meaning_ko: 메타 스킬 (다른 능력을 가능케 하는 능력)

key_phrases.phrase: in a way it simply wasn't
not because X, but because Y

topics.title_ko: 보통 주말에 무엇을 하는지 자세히 설명해 보세요 (활동·장소·함께하는 사람)

A2.title: I'd like to know what you usually do on the weekend. Tell me about a typical weekend in detail — what you do, where you go, and who you spend time with.
A2.body:
My weekend is usually quiet. On Saturday morning, I sleep late because I am tired from work. Then I have a slow breakfast at home. I drink coffee and read for a little while.

In the afternoon, I sometimes go for a walk in the park near my house. If the weather is good, I stay there for a long time. I also call my parents on Sunday. I talk to them for about an hour.

On Sunday evening, I clean my apartment and get ready for work. I do not do anything special, but it makes me feel calm.

B1.title: I'd like to know what you usually do on the weekend. Tell me about a typical weekend in detail — what you do, where you go, and who you spend time with.
B1.body:
My weekends are usually pretty quiet, and to be honest, I prefer them that way. On Saturday morning, I always sleep in because the week leaves me really tired. I'll usually have a slow breakfast at home, drink way too much coffee, and read something light for an hour or so.

In the afternoon, if the weather is good, I like to go for a walk in the small park near my apartment. Sometimes I meet a friend there for coffee, but often I just go alone, listen to a podcast, and let my mind rest.

Sundays are a little more routine. I call my parents in the afternoon — we usually talk for about an hour, mostly about nothing important. After that, I tidy up my apartment, do some laundry, and prepare a few things for the week ahead, like planning what I'll cook on Monday.

Nothing about my weekend is exciting, but that is exactly the point. By the time Sunday evening comes around, I feel rested and ready for another busy week.

key_phrases.meaning_ko: 또 한 번의 바쁜 한 주를 맞을 준비가 된

B2.title: I'd like to know what you usually do on the weekend. Tell me about a typical weekend in detail — what you do, where you go, and who you spend time with.
B2.body:
My weekends are usually pretty quiet, and honestly, I've come to appreciate them that way. The work week tends to leave me drained, so I treat Saturday and Sunday almost like a recovery routine.

On Saturday morning, the first thing I do is sleep in for as long as my body wants. After that, I'll have a slow breakfast at home — usually toast, eggs, and far too much coffee — and read something light for an hour or so. I try to stay off my work laptop completely, which is harder than it sounds.

In the afternoon, if the weather is reasonable, I like to take a long walk in the park near my apartment. Sometimes I meet a friend there for coffee, but more often I go alone with a podcast in my ears. It sounds a bit unsocial, but I find it genuinely restorative.

Sundays follow a slightly more structured rhythm. I usually call my parents in the afternoon, and we end up talking for about an hour about nothing particularly important. Afterwards, I tidy up the apartment, do laundry, and quickly plan the week ahead — what I'll cook, what I need to focus on at work, that kind of thing.

Nothing about my weekends is dramatic or exciting, but that's exactly the point. By Sunday evening, I usually feel calm and recharged, which makes it much easier to start a busy week without dreading it.

C1.title: I'd like to know what you usually do on the weekend. Tell me about a typical weekend in detail — what you do, where you go, and who you spend time with.
C1.body:
My weekends are usually pretty quiet, and honestly, I've come to appreciate them precisely for that reason. The work week tends to leave me genuinely drained, so I treat Saturday and Sunday almost as a deliberate recovery routine rather than a stretch of free time to be filled with activity.

On Saturday morning, the first thing I do is sleep in for as long as my body wants. After that, I'll have a slow breakfast at home — usually something simple like toast, eggs, and an indulgent amount of coffee — and read something light for an hour or so. I try to stay off my work laptop completely, although I have to admit that's much harder in practice than it sounds.

In the afternoon, weather permitting, I like to take a long walk through the small park near my apartment. Sometimes I'll meet a friend there for coffee, but more often than not I go alone with a podcast in my ears. It probably sounds a bit antisocial, but I find that kind of solo time genuinely restorative, and most of my closest friendships actually benefit from the fact that I show up well-rested rather than running on fumes.

Sundays follow a slightly more structured rhythm. I usually call my parents in the afternoon — we tend to talk for about an hour, mostly about nothing particularly consequential — and afterwards I'll tidy up the apartment, do laundry, and roughly plan the week ahead. That last bit is more important than it sounds: spending fifteen minutes thinking about what I'll cook and what I need to focus on at work makes Monday feel noticeably less chaotic.

There's nothing dramatic or particularly enviable about my weekends, but in a way that's exactly the point. By Sunday evening, I usually feel calm and quietly recharged, which makes it much easier to walk into another demanding week without immediately dreading it. I suspect that as I've gotten older, I've increasingly come to value that kind of unremarkable peace over the louder, more eventful weekends I used to chase.

key_phrases.meaning_ko: 점점 더 가치 있게 여기게 되었다
